title: The gathering knot
subtitle: Wrapping a bundle of cords to create a cylindrical column
excerpt: The gathering knot (also called the wrapping knot) binds a group of cords into a tight column by wrapping one cord many times around the bundle and securing both ends inside. It is the standard finishing knot for plant-hanger necks and cords that converge to a single point, and the standard technique for creating decorative cylindrical sections in wall hangings.
sourceNotes: The gathering (wrapping) knot is a standard macramé structure used for bundling and finishing cord groups. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A macramé finishing and structural knot formed by wrapping one cord many times around a bundle of cords and threading both ends inside the column. Also called the wrapping knot.
The single cord used to wrap around the bundle in a gathering knot. It is separate from the bundle cords and is hidden inside the column once complete.

content.text: wound tightly around a bundle. A loop is laid along the top of the bundle before wrapping begins; after wrapping, the working end is threaded through the loop and the loop is pulled down under the wrapping to lock both ends inside the column. The result is a smooth cylinder with no visible knot.

items.name: Cotton macramé cord, 3 mm single-strand
items.qty: 1 wrapping cord, 80 cm; bundle of at least 6 cords to practice on

steps: Hold the bundle of cords together. Lay the wrapping cord alongside the bundle with its tail pointing downward and a 5 cm loop formed at the top. The loop should run along the right side of the bundle.
Starting just below the loop, wrap the wrapping cord tightly around the bundle, moving downward. Each wrap sits close against the previous one.

Wrap to the desired column height (typically 2 to 4 cm for a plant-hanger neck; up to 6 cm for a decorative band). For each additional centimetre of column, allow approximately 6 to 8 cm of wrapping cord.
After the last wrap, thread the working end of the wrapping cord through the loop at the top of the column.
Pull the tail at the bottom of the column firmly downward. The loop tightens and draws the working end down inside the column, locking both ends.
Trim both the top tail and the bottom tail close to the wrapping. Neither end should be visible from outside the column.

content.text: A 2 cm column takes approximately 15 to 20 cm of wrapping cord on a bundle of 8 cords in 3 mm rope. A 4 cm column needs 30 to 35 cm. Cut the wrapping cord generously: excess is trimmed at the end; insufficient cord means unwrapping and starting again.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two common problems with gathering knots.

items.symptom: Loop pulls through the column completely and both ends come loose
items.cause: The loop was too small and slipped through when the tail was pulled.
items.fix: Leave a loop of at least 3 cm (the wrapping cord doubled back on itself should be thumb-width or wider). A larger loop grips as it is pulled down.

items.symptom: Column is lumpy rather than smooth
items.cause: Wraps were spaced apart rather than touching each other.
items.fix: Lay each wrap immediately against the previous one. Firm, consistent tension produces a smooth surface.
